texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon dioxide produced from the fossil fuels combustion in power plants is primarily responsible for the climate change [1,2]. Carbon dioxide capture from fl ue gas in power plant as an ' end-of-pipe ' technology has been considered as the greatest candidate for reducing CO2 emissions [3,4]. Membrane separation technology is widely recognized to be an alternative candidate to the traditional energy-intensive amine scrubbing technology for CO2 separation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: High-quality SAPO-34 membranes were fabricated by the secondary growth method on outside asymmetric tubular alumina supports. The e ff ects of temperature, pressure and feed fl ow rate on the separation performance of SAPO-34 membranes were investigated for CO2 separations from simulated fl ue gas. Single CO2, N2 and CH4 permeances were predicted by Maxwell-Stefan di ff usion model, and the predictions were fi t well with experimental data. The unexpected high CO2/N2 selectivities were 170 and 110 with high CO2 permeances of ~1.38 × 10 -6 mol/(m 2 s Pa) (=4120 GPU) through our SAPO-34 membrane for equimolar and 15/85 (vol/vol) CO2/N2 mixtures at 243 K and 0.1 MPa pressure drop, respectively. The membrane also displayed a CO2 permeance of 4.67 × 10 -7 mol/(m 2 s Pa) (=1390 GPU) and a CO2/N2 selectivity of 10.3 in a dry 15/85 (vol/vol) CO2/N2 mixture at 423 K. The moister in feed gas (0.68 mol% water vapor) decreased CO2 permeance and CO2/ N2 selectivity by only 19.3% and 14.9% in a wet 15/85 (vol/vol) CO2/N2 mixture at 423 K, respectively, compared to those in the dry mixture. The separation performance could be recovered to the original values after drying. The current SAPO-34 membranes were far beyond the upper bound of zeolite membranes in CO2/N2 plots and showed potential applications in CO2 separations from power-plant fl ue gas.

texts.orig: Polymer-based membranes were di ffi cult to be used directly in hightemperature fl ue gases. Several types of zeolite membrane materials have been investigated for CO2 separation from N2 due to their high performances as well as good chemical, mechanical and thermal stabilities. Kusakabe [22] et al. using an ion-exchanged zeolite FAU membrane for CO2/N2 separation; the K-exchanged zeolite NaY membrane displayed a CO2/N2 selectivity of 47.6 in a dry equimolar CO2/N2 mixture at 308 K. Guo et al. [17] reported a ZSM-5 membrane with a CO2/N2 selectivity of 68 at 293 K. Kim et al. [12] prepared an alumina disc supported h 0 h -oriented DDR membrane that has a CO2/N2 selectivity of 10 at 373 K in the wet mixture. Zhong et al. [46] fabricated an AlPO-17 membrane with a high CO2 permeance of 8.0 × 10 -7 mol/ (m 2 s Pa) and a CO2/N2 selectivity selectivity of 14 in an equimolar dry CO2/N2 mixture at 298 K. Wang et al. [49] prepared an AlPO-18 membrane that displayed a high CO2 permeance of 6.3 × 10 -7 mol/ (m 2 s Pa) and a good CO2/N2 selectivity of 45 at 298 K for a dry equimolar CO2/N2 mixture.

texts.orig: 34 membranes have been considered to be the most promising candidates for CO2 separation from fl ue gas and natural gas. Jang et al. [34] fabricated a disc alumina supported OSDA-free chabazite membrane with a CO2/N2 selectivity of 11 in a wet equimolar CO2/N2 mixture at 373 K. Li et al. [42] used SAPO-34 membrane for CO2/N2 separations; this membrane had a CO2 permeance of 7.7 × 10 -7 mol/(m 2 s Pa) with a CO2/N2 separation factor of 14 for a dry equimolar CO2/N2 mixture at 295 K using vacuum permeate pumping. Among these membranes, SAPO-34 membrane showed high CO2 permeance as well as reasonable CO2/N2 selectivity, which may due to its neutral and ions-free framework and competitive adsorption of CO2 over N2. Very recently, we have synthesized a tubular SSZ-13 membrane [33] with a CO2/N2 selectivity of 32 at 298 K in a dry equimolar CO2/N2 mixture. And we have also reported an OSDA-free chabazite membrane [35] with a CO2/ N2 selectivity of 14 at 473 K in a wet equimolar CO2/N2 mixture. But the permeances of the two membranes [33,35] were relatively low.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For CO2 separation from fl ue gas, it is necessary to treat typical hot (>373K) and wet CO2/N2 mixtures [4]. The membrane separation under wet and hot CO2/N2 mixtures were useful to estimate the potentials of membranes for direct CO2 capture from fl ue gas. However, the separations for an equimolar CO2/N2 mixture at room temperatures were reported in the literatures. There were little reports for the simulated flue-gas separations in a wet, hot and 15:85 (vol/vol) CO2/N2 mixture using membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this current study, we have reported the synthesis of high quality SAPO-34 membranes using homemade outside asymmetric alumina support tubes, and their separation performance in dry and wet CO2/N2 mixtures at di ff erent temperatures. The advantage of the current outside membranes is easier to scale up than our previous inside membranes [32] since the seeding on outer surface is simpler. Moreover, the ba ffl es could be installed in modules with outside membrane, but could not be done in modules with inside membrane. Another advantage of outside membrane is to su ff er from higher pressure than inside membrane.The unexpected high CO2/N2 selectivity of 110 together with good CO2 permeance of 1.38 × 10 -6 mol/(m 2 s Pa) were obtained at 243 K in a dry 15:85 (vol/vol) CO2/N2 mixture, respectively. The best membrane showed a CO2 permeance of 4.6 × 10 -7 mol/(m 2 s Pa) and a CO2/N2 selectivity of 10.3 in a dry 15:85 (vol/vol) CO2/N2 mixture at 423 K, respectively. Both permeance and selectivity decreased by only 19.3% and 14.9% when 0.68 vol% water vapor was introduced in the hot and 15:85 (vol/vol) CO2/N2 mixture, respectively. The membrane stability for separation in the hot and humid mixture was also evaluated. It showed that our membrane had potentials for the direct CO2 capture from fl ue gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Compared to our previous synthesis [32], synthesis procedure of SAPO-34 membrane was modi fi ed in order to make the synthesis cheaper. Most membranes were prepared using industrial-grade chemicals, and the membrane displayed the higher separation performance than our previous membranes [32]. The homemade outside alumina tubes were used for SAPO-34 membranes synthesis in the current study in replace of the inside alumina tubes from Inopor Co [32]. The homemade outside alumina tubes has an out diameter of 12 mm, an inner diameter of 9 mm, a wall thickness of 1.5 mm and an average pore size of 200 nm in top layer of outside tube. Unfortunately, the current commercial asymmetric alumina tubes for zeolite membrane preparation were micro fi lter membrane tubes and designed with inside-coating morphology. Therefore, most SAPO-34 membranes were synthesized using inside asymmetric alumina or stainless steel support tubes [32,41 -44,46,47,55,56]. The uniformity of seed coating on outer surface of tubular support is better than that on inner surface. For an inside membrane, the edge of glazing area could not be seeded uniformly.
texts.text: Compared to our previous synthesis [32], synthesis procedure of SAPO-34 membrane was modi fi ed in order to make the synthesis cheaper. Most membranes were prepared using industrial-grade chemicals, and the membrane displayed the higher separation performance than our previous membranes [32]. The homemade outside alumina tubes were used for SAPO-34 membranes synthesis in the current study in replace of the inside alumina tubes from Inopor Co [32]. The homemade outside alumina tubes has an out diameter of 12 mm, an inner diameter of 9 mm, a wall thickness of 1.5 mm and an average pore size of 200 nm in top layer of outside tube. Unfortunately, the current commercial asymmetric alumina tubes for zeolite membrane preparation were micro fi lter membrane tubes and designed with inside-coating morphology. Therefore, most SAPO-34 membranes were synthesized using inside asymmetric alumina or stainless steel support tubes [32,41 -44,46,47,55,56]. The uniformity of seed coating on outer surface of tubular support is better than that on inner surface. For an inside membrane, the edge of glazing area could not be seeded uniformly.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Cracks were easily yielded around the edge of the glazing. Furthermore, the growth environment of outside membrane is better than that of inside membrane, because the volume of the gel facing to the outer surface of support tube is much more than that facing to the inner surface. Therefore, the membrane quality of our outside SAPO-34 membranes could be improved even using industrial grade chemicals compared to our inside SAPO-34 membranes [32]. Please note that the membrane prepared using analytical reagents from Sigma-aldrich Co. displayed a little bit higher selectivities than our reported data of the membrane prepared using industrial grade reagents.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The crystal structure of the obtained powder particles and membrane samples were determined by X-ray di ff raction (XRD, Ultima IV) with Cu Ka radiation. The morphologies of SAPO-34 seed particles and membrane samples were detected by a fi eld emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-4800). Carbon dioxide and nitrogen adsorption of SAPO-34 crystals were conducted using an ASAP 2460 M (Micromeritics Instrument Corp.) adsorption apparatus at di ff erent temperatures. Prior to adsorption experiments, the calcined samples were degassed at a temperature of 473 K for 12 h under vacuum.

Carbon dioxide and nitrogen adsorption of SAPO-34 crystals were conducted using an ASAP 2460 M (Micromeritics Instrument Corp.) adsorption apparatus at di ff erent temperatures. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Permeance of single gas was performed using a dead-end system and mixture separations were carried out using a pressure-drop method. No sweep gas was used for both measurements. The detail description for the tests could be found in our previous study [31]. Separation was also carried out in a wet mixture containing 0.68 vol % water vapor in this study. Gas mixture was fl own through a temperature-controlled water bubbler by a water bath. The humidity of the feed mixture gas was constant at a certain water-bath temperature. Permeance and selectivity were used to record the separation performance of the membrane. A log mean pressure drop is used to calculate of the permeance. The detail formulations for the calculations of permeance and selectivity could be found in the literature [32] and also in the Supporting Information section. Separation index, π is determined by CO2 permeance times (selectivity - 1) times permeate pressure, which could evaluate comprehensively the membrane performance [57].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Adsorption isotherms of single CO2 and N2 on SAPO-34 at various temperatures and pressure up to 100 kPa are shown in Fig. 3. The adsorbed amount of CO2 on SAPO-34 was signi fi cantly larger than that of N2 within the test range of temperature and pressure. The CO2 loading and CO2/N2 adsorption selectivity were 2.6 mmol/g and 11.3 at 298 K and 100 kPa, respectively. Li et al. reported that CO2 loading and CO2/ N2 adsorption selectivity on SAPO-34 were 3.5 mmol/g and 20 at 295 K and 100 kPa, respectively [56]. A CO2 loading of 3.1 mmol/g and CO2/ N2 adsorption selectivity of 31 were obtained for a pure silica CHA zeolite [58]. Molecular simulation of the CO2 and N2 adsorption over silicalite-1 predicted that the CO2/N2 adsorption selectivity was approximately 19 [59]. Our experimental results were also consistent with the prediction [59] and the test results in the literatures [56,58,60].

texts.orig: 34 membrane M1 as a function of pressure drop at 298 K. Single CO2 and N2 permeances decreased with the increase of pressure. The former decreased greater than the latter, and thus the ideal CO2/N2 selectivity decreased with pressure. This membrane displayed single CO2 permeance of 1.48 × 10 -6 mol/(m 2 s Pa) with ideal CO2/N2 selectivity of 14.4 at 0.2 MPa and 298 K. Single CH4 permeance was almost independent of pressure. And therefore, ideal CO2/CH4 selectivity decreased faster than ideal CO2/N2 selectivity with the increase of pressure. The fl ux of a single gas through zeolite membranes can be attributed to the contributions by surface di ff usion, viscous fl ow and Knudsen di ff usion according to Eq. S(6) [61]. The permeance of CH4 which mainly went through nonzeolitic pores was less than 1.5 × 10 -8 mol/(m 2 s Pa), which indicated that this membrane contained very low concentration of defects (ideal CO2/CH4 selectivity of ~100). The unchanged CH4 permeance with pressure showed that the di ff usion of CH4 through few defects obeyed the Knudsen di ff usion (defect sizes was small than 2 nm [61,62]).

texts.orig: Fig. 5 illuminates the e ff ect of temperature on single-gas permeances and ideal selectivities of SAPO-34 membrane M1. Single CO2 and N2 permeances declined with temperature in the investigated temperature range, and CO2 permeance decreased faster. The faster downward trend for CO2 permeance than that of N2 permeance was due to that the coverage of preferential adsorpted CO2 specie on membrane surface decreased greater than that of N2 specie [31,32]. But single CH4 permeance almost kept constant because most of CH4 permeated through the nonzeolitic defects. Therefore, ideal CO2/CH4 selectivity decreased faster than CO2/N2 selectivity. The single-gas permeance that obeyed by adsorption-di ff usion mechanism always had a minimum because the permeation is dominated gradually by the di ff usion with the increasing temperature in a large range [63]. The minimum for single N2 permeance was observed in our temperature range. The minimum for CO2 permeance might be detected beyond 423 K based on the prediction [63].

texts.orig: The separation performance of di ff erent SAPO-34 membranes is highly dependent of the membrane thickness and amount of defects and support microstructure. Most SAPO-34 membranes were synthesized using inside asymmetric alumina or stainless steel support tubes. Li et al. used SAPO-34 membranes for CO2/N2 separations [42,56]. The inside asymmetric alumina tubular supported membrane had a CO2 permeance of 7.7 × 10 -7 mol/(m 2 s Pa) with a CO2/N2 separation factor of 14 for a dry equimolar CO2/CH4 mixture at 295 K using vacuum permeate pumping (feed pressure was atmosphere pressure) [42]. This membrane displayed a CO2 permeance of 1.2 × 10 -6 mol/ (m 2 s Pa) at a pressure drop of 0.1 MPa without vacuum. An inside asymmetric stainless steel tubular supported SAPO-34 membrane displayed an improved CO2/N2 selectivity of 39 by the incorporation of ethylenediamine, with relatively low CO2 permeance of 2.1 × 10 -7 mol/(m 2 s Pa) at 295 K and 138 kPa by Carreon et al. [43]. These previous SAPO-34 membranes had separation indexes of 0.79 -3.79 mol/(m 2 s). The average CO2 permeance and separation index of our current SAPO-34 membranes were 54 -780% and 44 -590% higher than those in the literatures [42,43,56], respectively.

texts.orig: Separation performances of SAPO-34 membrane M1 with pressure at room temperature in dry equimolar and 15:85 (vol/vol) CO2/N2 mixtures are shown in Fig. S3. The similar downward trends along with pressure were observed. The membrane displayed CO2 permeances of 1.82 × 10 -6 and 1.66 × 10 -6 mol/(m 2 s Pa), together with CO2/N2 selectivities of 32.9 and 29.8 in dry equimolar and 15:85 (vol/vol) CO2/ N2 mixtures, respectively. The CO2/N2 selectivities at 298 K was lower than those at 243 K. It was because that the loading of CO2 on SAPO-34 was lower than that of N2 at higher temperatures. And thus, the CO2/N2 selectivities of the membrane decreased based on the separation mechanism of adsorption-induced surface di ff usion. Similar results were reported for a b -oriented MFI zeolite membrane in CO2/H2 separations at temperature range of 238 -298 K [21]. The CO2/H2 selectivity decreased from 109 to 31 when temperature increased from 238 K to 298 K.

texts.orig: Separation performance of SAPO-34 membrane M1 along with pressure drop at 373 K in wet equimolar and 15:85 (vol/vol) CO2/N2 mixtures are shown in Fig. 7. The water vapor was introduced into the system by bubbling the water at room temperature. The mixture gas contained approximately 0.68 vol% water vapor. The wet mixture in the following sections contained the same water vapor by the same way. Carbon dioxide permeances and CO2/N2 selectivities decreased slightly with the increase of pressure in the two wet mixtures. The coverage of CO2 in the wet mixtures was lower than that in dry mixtures because that water preferentially adsorbed on zeolite surface inhibited the permeation of CO2 in the wet mixtures. Nitrogen permeances in the wet mixtures were higher than those in the dry mixtures because the lower CO2 permeances inhibited N2 permeances less. It is worth noting that the test condition (in the wet mixture and at high temperature) was close to the conditions for the actual fl ue gas [2,42]. It showed that our membrane displayed high separation performance and potential applications for direct CO2 capture from wet and hot flue gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 8 illuminates separation performance through SAPO-34 membrane M1 as a function of temperature in dry equimolar and 15:85 (vol/ vol) CO2/N2 mixtures at 0.1 MPa pressure drop. Carbon dioxide permeances had maxima of 2.20 × 10 -6 and 2.54 × 10 -6 mol/(m 2 s Pa) at 283 K in the two mixtures, respectively. Similarly, N2 permeances also had maxima at 323 K in both mixtures. Carbon dioxide/nitrogen selectivities decreased with temperature within the investigated temperature range. The maxima in CO2 and N2 permeances were rational based on a combinative e ff ect of adsorption and di ff usion, which is similar to the trends for other microporous zeolite membranes such as FAU [22] and MFI [63] membranes. At low temperatures (243 -283 K), the adsorption of CO2 was close to saturation and the surface coverage of CO2 changed less with the increase of temperature in this range. The increased di ff usivities of CO2 and N2 make the main contribution to the increasing amounts of the CO2 and N2 permeances. The surface coverages (adsorption capacity) of CO2 and N2 decreased quickly with the increase of temperature although the di ff usity increased, and thus the CO2 and N2 permeances had maxima in the test temperature range. When the temperature was as high as 423 K, the membrane still maintained CO2 permeances of 4.2 × 10 -7 and 4.6 × 10 -7 mol/ (m 2 s Pa) and CO2/N2 selectivities of 11.1 and 10.3 in dry equimolar and 15:85 (vol/vol) CO2/N2 mixtures, respectively. This temperature range of 373 -423 K was typical one of fl ue gas in supercritical bituminous power plants [42]. An alumina disc supported FAU membrane displayed a CO2/N2 separation factor of ~3 in a dry equimolar CO2/N2 mixture at 383 K [23]. A K-exchanged FAU membrane showed a CO2/ N2 of ~8at 373 K [22]. A h 0 h -oriented DDR membrane exhibited a CO2/N2 selectivity of ~10 in a dry equimolar CO2/N2 mixture at 373 K [12]. An alumina disc supported low-silica CHA (chabazite) membrane displayed a CO2/N2 of 8.8 in a dry equimolar CO2/N2 mixture at 373 K [34]. texts.orig: Separation performance of SAPO-34 membrane M1 as a function of temperature in wet equimolar and 15:85 (vol/vol) CO2/N2 mixtures are shown in Fig. 9. The trends with temperature in two mixtures were the same. The permeances of CO2 and N2 decreased with temperature in this temperature range were similar to those in the dry mixture in Fig. 8. As temperature increased in the wet mixture, CO2 permeance decreased greater than N2 permeance. And thus, the selectivity decreased with temperature. The downward trends of CO2 and N2 permeances were consistent with the results exhibited by the chabazite membranes [34]. The decreased permeances in the wet mixture were due to that the coverages of CO2 and N2 reduced by water adsorption on the zeolite surface. The addition of water vapor a ff ected separation performance of SAPO-34 membrane a little at high temperatures. For example, CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity of this membrane in the wet mixture decreased by only 19.3% and 14.9% in the 15:85 (vol/ vol) mixture at 423 K, respectively, compared to these in the dry mixture.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.5. Hydrothermal stability of SAPO-34 membrane in the humid CO2/N2 mixture
texts.text: 3.5. Hydrothermal stability of SAPO-34 membrane in the humid CO2/N2 mixture

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hydrothermal stability is a great issue that need to be considered in the separation using zeolite membrane materials because many industrial mixtures contained water vapor or steam. The temperature of power-plant fl ue gas without wet SO2 scrubber is 373 -423 K and the gas contains water vapor [42]. The long-term stability of SAPO-34 membrane M1 at 393 K was investigated in an equimolar CO2/N2 mixture containing 0.68 vol% water vapor as shown in Fig. 12. A relatively low feed fl ow rate of 1000 ml(STP)/min was used to save the feed gas despite that the sacri fi ce in separation performance occurred due to the e ff ect of concentration polarization ( in Fig. 10). The membrane M1 displayed stable CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity for a period of 120 h. When the supply of water vapor was stopped, the permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity of the dried membrane were recovered to the original values. It indicated that our SAPO-34 membrane had good longterm stability for CO2 capture from the simulated fl ue gas.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.6. Comparison to the literatures
texts.text: 3.6. Comparison to the literatures

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 13a compares our current SAPO-34 membrane M1 with the previous SAPO-34 membranes and other zeolite membranes listed in the CO2/N2 selectivity VS permeance plot. Most of the literatured data in Fig. 13a -c were tested at room temperatures. The data at 243 and 423 K for our membrane were also shown in Fig. 13 for comparison. The separation performances of zeolite membranes in Fig. 13 were also listed in Tables S3 -S4. MFI membrane with large pore structure (0.55 nm) displayed one of the highest CO2 permeance of 2.6 × 10 -6 mol/(m 2 s Pa), with a relative low CO2/N2 selectivity of 13.4 for an equimolar dry CO2/N2 mixture at 300 K [16]. The neutralframework of aluminophosphate-based molecular sieves membrane such as SAPO-34 [42,43], AlPO-17 [46] and AlPO-18 [49] membranes with small pore structure (0.38 nm) but strong a ffi nity towards CO2 exhibited both high CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 separation selectivity (as seen in Fig. 13 a). The e ff ect of humity on gas permeation for MFI (Si/Al > 10) and SSZ-13 (Si/Al > 10) membranes were smaller than that for FAU (Si/Al < 3) and low-silica CHA (Si/Al < 5) membranes, which was due to their stronger hydrophobic property. Our SAPO-34 membrane exceeded the CO2/N2 upper bounds of zeolite membranes at room temperatures. At 243 K, our membrane showed unexpected high selectivity with a high CO2 permeance. The separation index plot of these zeolite membranes for dry and wet CO2/N2 mixtures were shown in Fig. 13b and c, respectively. The current SAPO-34 membrane exhibited the highest separation index among the reported zeolite membranes in both dry and wet mixtures. It suggests that our SAPO-34 membranes have great potential applications in power-plant fl ue gas puri fi cation.

texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (1) High-quality CHA-type SAPO-34 membranes were fabricated on outside asymmetric tubular α -alumina supports after synthesis optimizations. The unexpected high CO2/N2 selectivities were 170 and 110 through our SAPO-34 membrane for equimolar and 15/85 (vol/vol) CO2/N2 mixtures at 243 K and 0.1 MPa pressure drop, respectively. The prediction by the Maxwell-Stefan di ff usion model was fi t well with experimental data for single-gas permeation.
texts.text: High-quality CHA-type SAPO-34 membranes were fabricated on outside asymmetric tubular α -alumina supports after synthesis optimizations. The unexpected high CO2/N2 selectivities were 170 and 110 through our SAPO-34 membrane for equimolar and 15/85 (vol/vol) CO2/N2 mixtures at 243 K and 0.1 MPa pressure drop, respectively. The prediction by the Maxwell-Stefan di ff usion model was fi t well with experimental data for single-gas permeation.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (2) The best membrane had a CO2 permeance of 1.82 × 10 -6 mol/ (m 2 s Pa) (=5430 GPU) with a CO2/N2 selectivity of 32.9 for an equimolar CO2/N2 mixture at 298 K and 0.1 MPa pressure drop. The separation performance was beyond the upper bounds of the current zeolite membranes in the CO2/N2 permeance VS selectivity plot. And the membrane had the highest separation index among zeolite membranes.
